content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 21 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 110r–111v (LC PDF pp. 218–219) | **Source Images:** DEU_021_page_001.jpg, DEU_021_page_002.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), p. 156 (pa_0156)

---

**1** If a slain person is found in the land that the LORD your God is giving you to possess, lying in the open field, and it is not known who struck him down, **2** then your elders and your judges shall go out and measure the distances to the cities that are around the body. **3** And the city that is nearest to the slain man—the elders of that city shall take a heifer of the herd that has not been worked, one that has not pulled in a yoke. **4** And the elders of that city shall bring the heifer down to a valley with running water, where no work has been done and nothing has been sown, and shall break the heifer's neck there in the valley. **5** Then the priests, the sons of Levi, shall come forward, for the LORD your God has chosen them to serve him and to pronounce blessing in the name of the LORD, and by their word every dispute and every assault shall be settled. **6** And all the elders of that city nearest to the slain man shall wash their hands over the heifer whose neck was broken in the valley, **7** and they shall declare and say, "Our hands did not shed this blood, nor did our eyes see it. **8** Accept atonement, O LORD, for your people Israel, whom you have redeemed, and do not set the guilt of innocent blood in the midst of your people Israel." And the bloodguilt shall be forgiven them. **9** So you shall purge the guilt of innocent blood from your midst, when you do what is right in the sight of the LORD.

**10** When you go out to war against your enemies, and the LORD your God gives them into your hand and you take them captive, **11** and you see among the captives a beautiful woman, and you desire her and want to take her as your wife, **12** then you shall bring her home to your house, and she shall shave her head and pare her nails. **13** And she shall take off the garments of her captivity and shall remain in your house and weep for her father and her mother a full month. After that you may go in to her and be her husband, and she shall be your wife. **14** But if you no longer delight in her, you shall let her go where she wants. But you shall not sell her for money, nor shall you treat her as a slave, since you have humiliated her.

**15** If a man has two wives, the one loved and the other unloved, and both the loved and the unloved have borne him children, and if the firstborn son belongs to the unloved, **16** then on the day when he assigns his possessions as an inheritance to his sons, he may not treat the son of the loved as the firstborn in preference to the son of the unloved, who is the actual firstborn. **17** But he shall acknowledge the firstborn, the son of the unloved, by giving him a double portion of all that he has, for he is the firstfruits of his strength. The right of the firstborn is his.

**18** If a man has a stubborn and rebellious son who will not obey the voice of his father or the voice of his mother, and, though they discipline him, he will not listen to them, **19** then his father and his mother shall take hold of him and bring him out to the elders of his city at the gate of his city. **20** And they shall say to the elders of his city, "This son of ours is stubborn and rebellious; he will not obey our voice; he is a glutton and a drunkard." **21** Then all the men of his city shall stone him to death with stones. So you shall purge the evil from your midst, and all Israel shall hear and be afraid.

**22** And if a man has committed a sin worthy of death and he is put to death, and you hang him on a tree, **23** his body shall not remain all night on the tree, but you shall bury him the same day, for a hanged man is a curse of God. You shall not defile your land that the LORD your God is giving you as an inheritance.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

**LOGOS — Formal Equivalence Analysis, Deuteronomy 21**

**v.1 "in the open field" (ba-sadeh):** Lit. "in the field." The implication of exposure in the open—not in a city where witnesses could be identified—is foundational to the ritual's logic.

**v.3 "heifer of the herd that has not been worked" (eglat bakar asher lo-uvad bah):** Eglah = heifer/young cow; the condition of never having been yoked is paralleled by the valley never having been sown (v.4). Both virgin states emphasize ritual purity for this atoning rite. LXX: δάμαλιν ἐκ βοῶν ἥτις οὐκ εἴργασται ("a heifer of cattle which has not worked").

**v.4 "valley with running water" (nachal eitan):** Nachal = wadi/stream-valley; eitan = "perennial/ever-flowing." The requirement of flowing water suggests purification (living water, as in Num 19). LXX: φάραγγα τραχεῖαν ("a rough/rugged valley"), missing the "perennial water" nuance. MT preferred.

**v.4 "break the heifer's neck" (araph):** The Qal of araph, used uniquely for this rite. Not a sacrifice—no blood offered on an altar, no fire. The neck-breaking releases the blood without ritual slaughter. LXX: νευροκοπήσουσιν ("they shall hamstring"). The MT verb is specific to this rite; LOGOS renders literally.

**v.6 "wash their hands" (rachatz et-yedeyhem):** The handwashing declares innocence (cf. Ps 26:6; Matt 27:24). The gesture is a legal-cultic declaration of non-participation in the bloodshed.

**v.8 "Accept atonement" (kapper):** The Piel imperative of kpr = to cover, atone, propitiate. The atonement is corporate, covering the whole community from bloodguilt. LXX: ἱλάσθητι ("be propitious/merciful"), capturing the petitionary aspect.

**v.11 "a beautiful woman" (eshet yefat to'ar):** Lit. "a woman beautiful of form." The phrase yefat to'ar recurs in the Bible for physical beauty. "Beautiful woman" is the standard rendering; "form" preserved by adding "beautiful" as the descriptor.

**v.12 "pare her nails" (aseta et-tsiporneyha):** LXX: περιελεῖ τὰς τρίχας αὐτῆς ("shall remove her hair"), which matches the shaving but then adds a different gloss. The Hebrew aseta for the nails means "make/trim/do." These mourning rites mark the transition from captive to wife. LOGOS follows MT.

**v.13 "garments of her captivity" (simlat shivyah):** Lit. "garment of her captivity." The clothes that marked her as a prisoner of war. LXX: τὰ ἱμάτια τῆς αἰχμαλωσίας αὐτῆς, matching MT.

**v.14 "humiliated her" (ineytah):** The Piel of anah = to humble, afflict, or—in sexual contexts—to violate. Here it acknowledges that the sexual union constituted a form of coercion upon the captive woman. The law grants her freedom and forbids her being sold as a slave in response to that acknowledged humiliation. LXX: ἐταπείνωσας αὐτήν ("you humbled her").

**v.15 "the one loved and the other unloved" (ahuvah u-senu'ah):** The Hebrew senu'ah ("hated") is softened to "unloved" in many versions. LOGOS uses "unloved" as a rendering that captures the comparative diminishment without requiring literal hatred, consistent with the context (cf. Gen 29:31 where Leah is "hated" = less loved).

**v.17 "double portion" (pi shnayim):** Lit. "mouth of two" = twice the share. The firstborn receives twice what each other son receives as his preeminent portion. LXX: διπλᾶ, matching MT.

**v.17 "firstfruits of his strength" (reishit ono):** The firstborn son represents the father's reproductive prime. "Firstfruits of his strength" is the established formal rendering (cf. Gen 49:3; Ps 78:51). LXX: ἀρχὴ τέκνων αὐτοῦ ("beginning of his children"), flattening the metaphor.

**v.18 "stubborn and rebellious" (sorer u-moreh):** The paired participial construction designates a legal category. The same pair in Jer 5:23; Ps 78:8. LXX: ἀπειθής ("disobedient") and ἐρεθιστής ("contentious"), splitting the concept.

**v.22-23 "a hanged man is a curse of God" (qilelat Elohim taluy):** This verse is foundational in later Jewish and Christian interpretation. Elohim here may be taken as: (a) a curse pronounced by God, (b) a divine curse, or (c) an insult to God (since the hanged man defiles the land God gave). Paul quotes this in Gal 3:13 as a typological lens on the crucifixion. LOGOS: "a curse of God" renders qilelat Elohim literally (the construct chain = "a cursing of God" = "a curse [that belongs to / proceeds from] God"). LXX: κεκατηραμένος ὑπὸ θεοῦ ("cursed by God"), making the agency explicit.

— LOGOS
